transcript.text: Hey, how's it going?
Good, how are you?
Doing well, thank you.
Ooh, nice microphone.
Thanks.
Got the whole spiel, three monitors, the headset, the microphone.
Now I just need a fourth one right up top here.
Do you podcast?
Yes.
So I used to do a lot of streaming back in the day until responsibility started to take over.
I still game at night, though, before bedtime.
But that's about it as far as streaming goes as of late.
Fair enough.
Fair enough.
That's very fun.
Cool.
Nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you.
I am based in Cleveland, Ohio, originally from Chicago, and then moved to Virginia, and then moved to Toledo, and then moved to Cleveland.
Cool.
We just had one of our directors just move to Ohio from Chicago.
Okay.
Love Love
Loves it, so.
Loves Ohio in comparison?
Yeah, well, I guess, like, he has, like, a bunch of their, his close friends live there, and they, like, recruited him to their neighborhood.
Okay.
Do you know where in Ohio?
I think Cleveland.
Cleveland?
Cleveland's, like, kind of like a mini Chicago.
Okay.
Well, maybe that's why he likes it.
Cool.
Well, I can kick us off and just give you a little bit of background on the role.
Am I the first person you're talking to after Tucker?
That is correct.
Yes.
That would be you.
Yeah.
So I can give you a bit of background on how we're thinking about the role.
It's, like, in the midst of changing, so this is kind of how we're thinking about it evolving.
Right.
And then we can talk about you, and then I'll leave time for questions at the end.
And these usually take about 30 minutes.
So.
I like we can use as much or as little of the time as we need to.
All right.
Cool.
So, yeah, as I mentioned, the account strategist role is evolving.
We operate as a marketing agency today, which means services model.
So we're generating, really just generating, like, organic blog content.
We don't do paid ads.
We don't do socials.
So, you know, like, video, all that stuff is, like, outside of kind of our bread and butter offering.
We generate all of our content using an internal platform that will eventually become external.
It basically leverages AI workflows, generates articles.
We do some editing and post-processing.
And then we're publishing about five articles a week.
And the way that the pods are going to work as we're changing things.
thank you.
is.

Is that we'll have like managing editors or content managers, kind of a variety of like role definitions based on seniority, generating and editing their own content and like developing those five articles a week for two to three clients per person.

And then account strategists will oversee eight or nine accounts and they'll be responsible for weekly meetings, kind of just making sure that the project is on track, they're coordinating everything.
They're like, if we're thinking about health scoring, they're the owner of like the relationship and like the contractual output, like meeting the scope of work.
They will be working with, there is a layer of like directors who are really specialized.
in like SEO, AEO, kind of like strategic operations.

And that team will essentially be a pool of like floating experts that can hop in and help with like changing strategic direction or like doing a little bit more like deeper project work as needed to support different clients.
So we're not assigning directors to clients.
The account strategist really owns the book of business, everything associated with that.
They have managing editors who are generating content, and then the directors will help with like strategic work on top of that.
Gotcha.
Any questions?
No, that all seems pretty straightforward.
think that's one of the questions that I originally had with Tucker that he mentioned that either you or Bridget may have a little bit more insight on.
So that's awesome understanding that essential layer in terms of where the account strategist would be injected into.
Yeah, yeah, so you're like, as I said, kind of owning that.

And then thinking about renewals, we're not really doing upsells or additional work streams, it's not so much like a sales position as like a kind of CS more position, but with that, obviously, like marketing expertise and piece, that's really important to be able to present as experts when talking to clients.
Cool.
So I would love to learn a little bit more about you, get your elevator pitch, and then I have some questions.
Awesome.

So on a personal front, I do have my bachelor's in psychology with a minor in speech-language pathology and a master's in clinical mental health counseling, so a lot of my education can tie into a lot of that account strategy and speaking with customers because I love listening, active listening more to SEO because sometimes they just want to talk, they just want to talk about their goals, their KPIs, and things like that, and then I come in with the follow-up and how does...
...accommodate for them.

As far as my professional experience, currently right now, I'm at Kuno Creative as the SEO program manager, tying in with AEO, GEO, and essentially, I am the main POC on essentially all of the accounts.
I have been on sales pitches.
And when I'm on those sales pitches, I end up taking over that account from start to finish, execution, strategy, implementation.
And then I also play a role in touching base with content editors, brand journalists, depending on the needs of the client, whether that's blogs, pillar pages, cluster pages, things of that nature.
I also play a role when it comes to renewals, because since I am the SME within that specific designated field of SEO, I speak to essentially every single part of that.
Prior to Kuno Creative, I held positions in a leadership...
So at Neil Patel Digital, at Grow, which Kirkland actually was at Grow, and he had said really awesome things about GrowthX, and also Logical Position, I was managing there.

So I had a team of anywhere between 5 to 9 SEO account managers, or SEO strategists slash analysts, where I essentially oversaw anywhere between 70 to 90 accounts, because their accounts are essentially mine.
And a lot of them manage anywhere between 20 to 40 accounts.
So times 5, times 9, I pretty much own a lot of that.
Held a lot of one-on-ones, held a lot of group meetings to help build morale.
And essentially, any fires that needed to be put out, I was there to essentially be the firewoman in that case.
So the reason why I switched from a leadership role into the SEO program manager role now, which I'm not managing.
Anyone, it's a very small team, me and two other SEOs.
So we pretty much own ourselves and we pretty much guide each other.
So I wanted to be more in that strategic role because I do love managing.
I love leadership.
I love overseeing individuals and understanding their strengths, their weaknesses, and how to mold them into areas where they feel like they may not perform well.
And leadership is great, but I wanted to be the reason why we're seeing that growth.
A lot of the calls that I was on is more putting out fires, any needs that the client may have or any concerns.
I was there, but I want to be doing the work.
I want to implement the work and see those results instead of being retroactive.
Awesome.
That's a helpful clarifying point.
So can you tell me about an account that you manage that requires support from multiple players?
within your company.
mentioned that you take the lead a lot and have a couple of different points of contact, but how does the team come together to support a client?

Yeah, so where we're at right now, we have several accounts that not only have SEO or content or paid or media, but specifically talking about content specifically, when it comes with SEO and content, I'm not very much of a content writer.
However, I have a lot of the knowledge and the expertise in terms of choosing the right keywords, phrasing the appropriate headers based on what people are searching for.
And when it comes to a team, I need to rely on the content writer or the brand journalist in terms of, Hey, you know, here's the ideas that I have.
Here's the outline that I'm wanting to convey.
Let's work together in working with the outline that
That I have with the chosen keywords that I've produced based on my research and let's put together a draft.
So it's more understanding who's on the team and what each other's strengths are and leaning on them for that.
Like me, keywords, strategy, headers, outlines, I can do that.
But as far as the context in terms of the content for each of those headers and paragraphs, like I'm going to lean on the content writer for that.
Yeah.
Awesome.
And then how do you think about like project management?
So organizing all of the different people, even when you're tapping different, like different people for different skill sets.
Yeah.

So I think in terms of project management and juggling through a multitude of those, like let's say I have eight to nine accounts with multiple clients reaching out to me, a lot of tasks coming down the pipeline.
I think it's all about, it's all about deadlines.
Like I think if there's a specific deadline to accomplish and the severity.
I'm sure.
you.
And the complexity of each task, it's about following through.
I think the biggest thing is communication.
If there are eight to nine different accounts and several tasks open, I think it's going through each single one and touching base to at least generate a comment like, hey, this is where we're at.
So it's not leaving one project left behind, but it's going through each single one of those.
With project management, I would say that I'm very, very organized because I tend to over communicate as far as like, hey, this is where we're at.
If I haven't heard in X, Y, Z days, I do a friendly touch base.

If it's like with a team member or clients, myself personally, when someone tags me or reaches out to me, I respond not immediately, but within a really good time frame to be like, hey, at least I saw this.
So it all starts with communication, I feel like with project management and organization.
Awesome.
And how do you structure your week when managing multiple accounts?
Accounts too.
So it's like there's one side of it, which is managing people within a project.
And then the other side is how do you structure your work week across multiple deadlines, making sure that nothing gets missed?
Yeah.
In that sense, I really like to utilize the calendar a lot.
I like to block off time specifically.
So I don't use my calendar specifically just for meetings, but I use the calendar to set specific tasks on what I need to do or what I need to focus on.
For example, if there's like a monthly report coming up, I'll block like an hour on X day to be able to accommodate for that in advance.
Setting deadlines is actually very, very important.

So for every project that there is, I believe that every task should have at least some sort of deadline to at least project when that needs to be accomplished and when that needs to be taken a look at.
And do you use any other tools for project management?
Um, if so right now internally, like with the.
With the Kuno Creative that I'm at right now, I just use my calendar, and then we use Basecamp for our system in terms of project management.
Okay.
And do you communicate internally and externally in Basecamp?
Yep, internally and externally in Basecamp.
Okay, and then I haven't used Basecamp.
Can you track projects there too?
like task-oriented?
Yep.
Awesome.
Can you talk to me about a time when a client deliverable was going off track and you had to bring the client back?
Yeah.
So trying to think of a situation in where that has essentially happened.
Okay, so I do have a current account where we had a content writer on board, and we had to reduce the scope because of the tariffs going on earlier this year.
And so we no longer had a content writer.
And with that being said, we were generating glossary pages because that's really important as far as information, AEO, GEO, really good support to suffice for why glossary pages are important.
So sent over a glossary page on a specific term.
And basically, the content on there had nothing to do with the product that they are producing.

So what I had to do was I had to hop on the call with the clients, essentially explain the situation that we're in, and basically had mentioned like, hey, know, without the content writer, you know, I'll be very transparent.
I'm not much of a writer.
There are tools that we can utilize like Surfer SEO.
We do use that.
So I'm very transparent in the tools that we use in relation to AI.
And I said, hey, let's take a step back.
Let's repurpose the content instead of a glossary page, which is much more informative.
Informational in nature.
This is more like Wikipedia style.
Let's repurpose the content and turn it into a blog that's more educational.
And we can tie it back to one of your pillar pages that you do have.
Throughout that entire process, it took about three weeks because, again, I'm not much of a writer, so I'm doing the very best that I can.
And they understood that.
And we actually just published the page last Wednesday as a blog.
So that was a little stressful because we generated a whole glossary page that's actually quite lengthy and had a lot of the modules related to bullet points, tables, accordions, and tab layouts.
And we had to essentially scrap all of that.
What's your account management philosophy?
Yeah.
Trying to understand the question a little bit more.
Yeah.
So, you know.
What's like your North Star when you're thinking about managing accounts?
How do you approach either communication or are you thinking deliverables are number one?
What do you orient yourself towards as you're getting to know accounts?
It would be the relationship and the transparency with myself as well as the point of contact that I'll be working with as far as the account.

Because if there's no trust and there's no relationship, like if they don't feel I'm a best fit for them and I don't feel we're a best fit either, then the relationship's probably not going to go well as far as sending deliverables, getting the message across, talking through strategy.
So I think it's all about relationship that like for me, that's the North Star.
It's making sure that they're comfortable and they trust me and it goes kind of like both ways.
Yeah.
What do you do to go about building that?
Yeah.
So in terms of building...

That relationship with the client, it's asking the right questions, being very invested in terms of their goals, their KPIs, their needs, their wants, the challenges that they've faced, and being able to put together a solution and a game plan to essentially accommodate for all of their needs.
So it's always going to be like with SEO, everything's kind of like trial and error.
And a lot of the times, if there's a question or something that I don't know, I'll be very transparent and let them know like, hey, like, that's a really good question.
That's something I'm going to have to follow up on.
But here's some insight that I do have.
And I can follow up with that with more research.
So it's just understanding.
It's listening to them.
It's asking the right questions.
Because most of the time as a marketer, I think sometimes we might do a lot of talking, but sometimes we should let the client do most of the talking because we can grab a lot from that.
Yeah.
Um, I want to go back a little bit to like prioritization and organization.
an
And you talked about using calendars, using Basecamp.
I think one thing that we are thinking through right now, right, is like eight to nine clients.
You're talking to all of those clients on a weekly basis.
Sometimes deadlines are going to bump into each other, overlap.
Like, how do you think about coordination and organization?
Do you have an example of when you worked on a couple of tight deadlines all at once, when you had to, like, really, you know, orchestrate everything together?

So if there's a lot of tasks and a lot of deadlines competing with one another, I think instead of looking at everything as a whole and overwhelming oneself, it's looking at each one to see maybe one is less complex in terms of pushing the ball down the pipeline.
Because while managing projects, it's about keeping things going, right?
So if there's a role,
Roadblock that can be easily addressed versus maybe the third or fourth task that there is, it's kind of hitting those one at a time.
Looking at everything as a whole, seeing what the roadblock is, seeing which roadblock is easy to move at least one step further, and then waiting to hear back, and then moving on to the next one.
So I think it might be a case-by-case basis, but that's how I would approach it.
Do you have any examples?
I'm trying to think of a specific example that I do have.
Right now, I'm currently managing 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 14 accounts.
And with all of those accounts, actually, every morning, I essentially look through each of those accounts.
And in Basecamp, there is a specific my stuff and my assignments with specific due dates.
And I literally go through all of the to-dos to see if there's anything that I need to
to be doing at that point in time, based on that due date.

But honestly, for the past two and a half months that I've been at Kuno Creative, I can't recall a very specific situation where a lot of the deadlines were impeding one another that it became overwhelming.
Okay.
Cool.
Well, those are all the questions I have.
What questions do you for me?
Awesome.
Let us see.
I'm very curious to hear, you know, what types of tools the account strategist would use regularly as far as, like, project management, communication, tracking, reporting, analytics dashboard.
Like, from our side, I come more from, like, an SEO background.
So a lot of, like, SEMrush, Ahrefs, Moz, Surfer SEO, things like that.

primarily on like the keyword, like gap analysis, things like that.
And then we have access to the clients like GSC, GA4 for our own tracking on like progress.

We use Linear for tracking engineering tickets.

then a lot of like client communications happen between Slack and then documents that we're sharing with them are in Notion in terms of kind of artifacts that are going into the AI and then Google Docs for like Docs for them to review.
Perfect.
Looking at some of the clients that GrowthX is currently working with, what are some of their core challenges when they're thinking about partnering with growth?
So let's say there's like a prospect and they're talking with GrowthX.
What are their core challenges that they're focused on?
Is it like SEO, AI-driven searches, GEO, target audience, content gap analysis, CRO, all of that?
I would say all of that.
I think the big question or the big point that's coming up most often right now is AEO.
So like AI visibility, making sure that they're like optimized for that, which, you know, combination of refreshes and new content.
And the, I would say like the challenge for us is that like walking the line between that like optimized content and like beautiful writing.
You know, like beautiful writing is important if like your end goal is like showing up in search and being.
And some clients have a really hard time wrapping their head around that.
And efficiency, getting content out and getting signals is going to get them closer to their goal.
But they really can't see that the forest for the trees of this article has a spelling error.
Gotcha.
So it's a lot of the content that GrowthX is building for the clients.
So the way that it works, as my understanding, is the content gets produced by AI.
And then there's like a content editor manager that essentially cross-references everything like, you know, double EAT, making sure that it's all kosher as far as what the client's voice is.
And then it gets sent to the account strategist to send over or what's kind of like the pipeline.
Yeah.
So the managing editor or content manager is generating the content in the AI.
Right.
They're going going through.
They're
So post-processing and like perplexity and clod.
And then we also, our platform, we are developing like our own model in the platform to like get feedback and edit content internally.
I think the biggest challenge on that, or like the way that that works with going back and forth is like right now there's a second layer of editing.
And then the client has like three points of contact because like depending on the pod, different people are talking to the client.
We are moving towards a model where you would be the person that like you can communicate everything like you're not generating the content, but you might be doing like the gap analysis.
And then we work in Airtable to update the client as well.
So you might say, hey, there's links to content for you to review in Airtable, but you're not doing the editing, the generation, any of that.
Awesome.
All right.
Let's see.

Like, do you see a lot of trends as far as like deliverable quality, the timeliness of sending the deliverables, and how is that being addressed?
Yeah, that goes back to what I was saying earlier about like the stated goals versus like them wanting perfect content.
Yeah.
So that I think is like the balance of some of the like messaging, especially that will be the role of the account strategist to be like, look, this isn't like what you want to do.
Like signed on with GrowthX for, to get like content out there.
Like, let's look at.
And let's publish and not go back and forth and aim for perfection.
I think that's like the hardest part.
And it's really hard to get clients to let go of that.
I'm experiencing that a lot.
The other thing that will be super important for this person is that relationship building.

We have relationships with our main points of contact, but we want somebody who's going to think about the expansion of like sort of a land and expand mindset of like, are we talking not just to the editor of our content or like the approver on the publishing side?
But are we making sure that we're keeping the decision maker and the budget holder read in?
Are we actually like making progress on the trust of like what growthx is delivering at a decision maker level?
Right.
Makes sense.
Awesome.
Let's see here.
So given like my background specifically in SEO, AEO, and GEO, how do you see my experience fitting into this account strategist role?

Yeah, I mean, I like that you can speak to all of those pieces because tying the strategy that the directors are coming up with to kind of the work that we're doing and being able to kind of keep that connection between, you know, this is what we're, this is the high level goal.
This is what we're aiming at.
This is like the full strategic direction.
Here's the content that we're delivering.
You being able to speak to that from that like SEO, AEO perspective, and talk about changes that are happening in the space, things like that.
That's super important.
Yeah.
I mean, I think in our conversation just now, I'll be honest, I was hoping for more specific examples from you on each of those pieces, but like.
Like, do think, you know, there's, you, you seem to have the experience, so.
Yeah, that's, that's the tricky part of, is getting examples within the timeframe that's recent.

So that's one of the things.

So I have to put in an alarm just in case.
Yeah.
Awesome.

So since GrowthX is mainly remote focused, how is GrowthX kind of helping to build morale?
Are there any, like, it might be hard to do team outings, but maybe there's something like virtual that sometimes happens, you know, talk to me a little bit about that.
Yeah, I mean, I, I will be honest that that's certainly.
No,
That's weak point for us at this point.
We have like biweekly all hands.
And we have a global team.
So it's not just remote in the US.
It's like, you know, people are in Pakistan and Croatia and like all over the world, Philippines, Singapore, like everywhere.
So it's really hard to be like, hey, everybody, let's get online at 3 a.m.
your time for Q&A.
Yeah, that might be a little hard.
Yeah, we like we do ask that everybody overlap with the West Coast for four hours a day.
But, you know, during those hours, it's people are trying to like coordinate with their team members and get their work done.
So I would say, like, we have a lot of Slack channels, and people are really active, like, on the water cooler Slack channel, and there's like a book club Slack channel and a cooking Slack
So I think like people are interact that way, but there's not a lot of like FaceTime, if you will, just like chatting.
You know, we're also in kind of a crazy phase of growth where there's like a lot to get done at this point.
As expected.
Yeah.
As far as kind of like looking at training and mentorship and conference budgets and, you know, kind of getting certifications, is that something that growthx supports?
For example, if let's say there's a really awesome training on like AI that I'm interested in that ties into to SEO and all of the talks on that, would that be supported?
On a case-by-case basis, I think, yes.
Like, I've, people haven't like brought it up.
People have asked about like that before in their onboarding.
I haven't, I mean, I haven't been.
For six months, though, it hasn't been that long.
But like, I know that we have said, like, yes, if on a case by case basis, if something seems like it would be relevant to the role, certainly.
And then, like, how have you been liking it so far?
Like, what are your highlights and maybe challenges or areas of opportunity?
Yeah, I mean, I think our business model is really cool in that it gives us space to run and we're developing the product on the back end.
We're making the product better by delivering for clients and we're able to bill and pay for ourselves, basically, through our, like, invoicing and, like, our services.
So that part, having, like, been at startups that did not do that is, like, a nice to have.
And then the, I think, like, the challenge is just...
Like we are growing super fast and the like capacity, like I'm reorging the team right now, we're redefining these roles to try and like find leverage in different places.
It's a lot of change.
I mean, it's like, so there's a lot of like management of that on a people front to be like, okay, this is, you know, it's for the good of the company.

It's for like that benefit, but it's, you know, a lot of like, hey, how do I make sure that like people have some work life balance, but like we are making these promises to these clients and we're like, there's a lot of, you know, a lot of work to be done.
Yeah.
It's a good problem to have sometimes.
Yeah, totally.
Awesome.
I don't think at this point I have any other questions.

Awesome.
Sweet.

You too.
